skill.name: systematic-debugging
skill.description: Guides root cause analysis for bugs, flaky tests, and repeated fix failures using evidence gathering and focused verification.
skill.summary: Debug software issues by tracing root causes before applying fixes.
skill.author: CodingCossack

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but review found most are false positives from Markdown diagrams, fenced examples, and ordinary debugging prose. The confirmed risk is an included Bash helper that runs project test commands and checks filesystem state, which is useful but should be treated as code execution in the target repository.

medium_findings.title: Helper Script Executes Repository Test Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with legitimate intent. The Bash helper enumerates test files and runs npm test for each file, so using it executes the target repository test command and any configured test hooks. This is useful for finding state pollution but should only be run in trusted repositories or isolated environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution is direct and observable in the script. Confidence is high that it runs project-controlled code, but the surrounding usage text shows debugging intent rather than malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Diagnostic Examples May Expose Local Context in Logs
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE but low severity. The documentation suggests printing environment variable presence, current working directory, and stack traces during debugging. This can reveal local paths or secret names in logs, but the examples do not transmit data externally and are meant for local diagnosis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines clearly show diagnostic logging of environment presence, cwd, and stack details. The impact is limited because no network sink or credential value printing was found.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE grouping. Many reported Ruby backtick and weak cryptography findings are fenced Markdown diagrams, prose, timeout examples, or words inside documentation rather than executable Ruby or cryptographic code. No evidence found of malware, exfiltration, prompt injection, or hidden network behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms these locations are documentation snippets or ordinary TypeScript timeout errors. The scanner appears to have matched Markdown fences and substrings without semantic execution context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The find-polluter helper invokes npm test on discovered test files. This pattern is expected for a debugging utility but can execute arbitrary package test scripts from the repository under inspection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line directly invokes npm test with a discovered file path. Risk depends on the trust level of the repository being debugged.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem State Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper checks whether a target file or directory exists and prints details with ls. This is a normal debugging pattern, but it can disclose local file metadata in terminal output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem checks are explicit and local. They are not malicious, but they are relevant because users choose the path being inspected.

content.value_statement: Complex bugs often survive quick fixes because the original cause is still unknown. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through evidence gathering, tracing, hypothesis testing, and focused verification.
actual_capabilities: Guides step-by-step root cause investigation before code changes.
Helps trace bad values backward through call chains and test setup.
Provides condition-based waiting patterns for flaky asynchronous tests.
Recommends validation layers after a confirmed root cause is found.
Includes a helper script for identifying tests that create unwanted state.
Defines escalation signals after repeated failed fixes.
limitations: Does not automatically diagnose bugs without access to project evidence.
The Bash helper runs repository test commands and should be used with trusted code.
Examples are generic and may need adaptation for each language or framework.
It cannot replace domain knowledge for complex architecture decisions.

use_cases.title: Investigate a Failing Test Suite
use_cases.description: Trace intermittent failures to timing, shared state, or setup order before changing test code.
use_cases.target_user: QA engineers and test automation developers

use_cases.title: Debug Production-Like Regressions
use_cases.description: Collect evidence across component boundaries and identify the layer where data first becomes invalid.

use_cases.title: Recover from Repeated Failed Fixes
use_cases.description: Stop patch stacking, form a single testable hypothesis, and decide when architecture needs review.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Debugging Investigation
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a new bug report.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic debugging on this issue. First summarize the symptom, reproduction steps, observed errors, and missing evidence before proposing any fix.

prompt_templates.title: Trace the Root Cause
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for a bug with a stack trace.
prompt_templates.prompt: Trace this failure backward from the visible error. Identify each caller, each important value, and where the bad value first appears.

prompt_templates.title: Stabilize a Flaky Test
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for timing or async failures.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this flaky test for arbitrary waits. Replace timing guesses with condition-based waiting and explain what condition each wait proves.

prompt_templates.title: Review Failed Fix Attempts
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt after multiple unsuccessful patches.
prompt_templates.prompt: We have tried several fixes without resolving the issue. Audit the evidence, list untested assumptions, form one new hypothesis, and recommend the smallest verification step.

output_examples.input: A test sometimes fails because expected tool results are missing after a short timeout.
output_examples.output: The response should identify the timeout as a symptom, ask what event proves completion, and recommend waiting for that event count instead of sleeping.

output_examples.input: A command creates a file in the source directory during tests.
output_examples.output: The response should trace the directory value backward, inspect setup order, find the first empty or wrong path, and propose validation at multiple layers.

output_examples.input: Three patches have introduced new failures without fixing the original bug.
output_examples.output: The response should stop further patching, summarize evidence gaps, challenge the current design assumption, and request human review if architecture is implicated.

best_practices: Reproduce the issue and read the full error before proposing fixes.
Change one variable at a time and verify the hypothesis with the smallest test.
Add validation at each layer after the true source of invalid data is found.
anti_patterns: Do not stack multiple fixes before confirming the root cause.
Do not replace flaky sleeps with longer arbitrary sleeps.
Do not log secret values while adding diagnostic instrumentation.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps investigate bugs, flaky tests, and unexpected behavior by finding the root cause before applying fixes.

faq.question: Does it write code automatically?
faq.answer: It guides the debugging process. Code changes should come after evidence confirms a specific cause.

faq.question: Can it help with flaky tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes condition-based waiting patterns that replace unreliable sleeps with checks for real completion states.

faq.question: Is the included shell script safe to run?
faq.answer: Run it only in trusted repositories or isolated environments because it executes the project test command.

faq.question: Does it support Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is described for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: When should I stop debugging and escalate?
faq.answer: Escalate after repeated failed fixes, when each fix reveals deeper coupling, or when the architecture assumption looks wrong.
